content.full_text:
M-80-154

Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    March 14, 1980




John Giansello
Mass Mailings


     The United Professors of California is a labor organization. It
sponsors a political action committee which solicits its funds directly
from individual members of the union. None of its funds come from the
union treasury.

     The union itself wishes to send out a mailing to all members on
insurance and pension matters. None of this material will involve
political purposes as we define them.

     The political action committee has prepared and paid for the
printing of a circular to oppose Proposition 9.

     The political action committee wishes the union to include a copy
of the political circular in each envelope mailed to members in con-
nection with insurance and pension matters. The union is agreeable and
will pay for the envelopes and postage, as well as its own enclosures.

     Who is the sender? (If the entire mailing is counted, the union
will bear the major portion of the cost.)

     What is the mass mailing, the entire package or just the political
enclosure and any incremental postal charge attributable to the in-
clusion of the political material? Or should the cost of the envelopes
also be pro-rated by the relative length, volume or weight of the
contents?

     What must be on the outside of the envelope?

     Our requirements for mass mailings in Government Code §84305 apply
only to persons who make "expenditures" 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18225(c)(3)
talks about expenditures that are "reportable" by persons other than
those described in 18225(b) who make payments for communications which
expressly advocate the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly
identified candidate or candidates, or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure (our multi-purpose
committees). The union in this case would be such a multi-purpose
committee if it made such payments. By 18225(c)(3) the only parts of
the payments that are reportable by a multi-purpose committee are
those directly attributable to the political communication. Regular
ongoing business overhead which will be incurred in similar amounts

Opinion Request Meeting                    -2-                    March 14, 1980
Participants


regardless of the political communication is not part of the expenditure for a multi-purpose committee.

     Here, if the enclosure makes the entire package political, the union will be making an expenditure, and because it is paying the larger portion of the whole the union will be the sender.

     If the insurance and pension materials, on the other hand, are "regular ongoing business overhead" - including envelope and postage, then the union will be making no "expenditure."  The only "expenditures" made will be by the PAC and the mass mailing requirement would seem to apply only to the PAC and its costs. Even if there is an incremental postal charge attributable to the enclosure, paid by the union, the PAC will be paying the larger portion of the political costs and will be the sender.

sections.question: The union wishes to send a mailing to members on insurance and pension matters. The political action committee has prepared and paid for printing of a circular to oppose Proposition 9 and wishes the union to include a copy in each envelope. The union will pay for envelopes and postage. Who is the sender? What is the mass mailing - the entire package or just the political enclosure? What must be on the outside of the envelope?
sections.conclusion: If the insurance and pension materials are 'regular ongoing business overhead' including envelope and postage, the union makes no 'expenditure.' The only expenditures made will be by the PAC, and mass mailing requirements would apply only to the PAC and its costs. Even if there is an incremental postal charge attributable to the enclosure paid by the union, the PAC will be paying the larger portion of the political costs and will be the sender.
sections.question_synthetic: A labor union and its political action committee wish to jointly distribute a mailing where the union covers insurance/pension materials and postage costs while the PAC covers political advocacy material costs. The question asks how to determine who is the sender, what constitutes the reportable 'mass mailing,' and what disclosure requirements apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that if the union's insurance and pension mailing constitutes regular ongoing business overhead, the union is not making a reportable political expenditure. The PAC would be the sender and subject to mass mailing requirements only for costs directly attributable to the political enclosure.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document dated March 14, 1980. Contains opinion request meeting discussion with FPPC analysis of expenditure and mass mailing requirements under Gov. Code §84305 and Cal. Admin. Code §18225. No formal 'CONCLUSION' or 'QUESTION' headers present; content extracted from narrative discussion. Some OCR errors present in original document ('Meetinq' for 'Meeting', 'orqanization' for 'organization'). The response discusses multi-purpose committee reporting requirements and the distinction between political expenditures and regular business overhead.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The union wishes to send a mailing to members on insurance and pension matters. The political action committee has prepared and paid for printing of a circular to oppose Proposition 9 and wishes the union to include a copy in each envelope. The union will pay for envelopes and postage. Who is the sender? What is the mass mailing - the entire package or just the political enclosure? What must be on the outside of the envelope?

CONCLUSION: If the insurance and pension materials are 'regular ongoing business overhead' including envelope and postage, the union makes no 'expenditure.' The only expenditures made will be by the PAC, and mass mailing requirements would apply only to the PAC and its costs. Even if there is an incremental postal charge attributable to the enclosure paid by the union, the PAC will be paying the larger portion of the political costs and will be the sender.
embedding.first_500_words: M-80-154 Opinion Request Meeting Participants March 14, 1980 John Giansello Mass Mailings The United Professors of California is a labor organization. It sponsors a political action committee which solicits its funds directly from individual members of the union. None of its funds come from the union treasury. The union itself wishes to send out a mailing to all members on insurance and pension matters. None of this material will involve political purposes as we define them. The political action committee has prepared and paid for the printing of a circular to oppose Proposition 9. The political action committee wishes the union to include a copy of the political circular in each envelope mailed to members in con- nection with insurance and pension matters. The union is agreeable and will pay for the envelopes and postage, as well as its own enclosures. Who is the sender? (If the entire mailing is counted, the union will bear the major portion of the cost.) What is the mass mailing, the entire package or just the political enclosure and any incremental postal charge attributable to the in- clusion of the political material? Or should the cost of the envelopes also be pro-rated by the relative length, volume or weight of the contents? What must be on the outside of the envelope? Our requirements for mass mailings in Government Code §84305 apply only to persons who make "expenditures" 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18225(c)(3) talks about expenditures that are "reportable" by persons other than those described in 18225(b) who make payments for communications which expressly advocate the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or candidates, or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure (our multi-purpose committees). The union in this case would be such a multi-purpose committee if it made such payments. By 18225(c)(3) the only parts of the payments that are reportable by a multi-purpose committee are those directly attributable to the political communication. Regular ongoing business overhead which will be incurred in similar amounts Opinion Request Meeting -2- March 14, 1980 Participants regardless of the political communication is not part of the expenditure for a multi-purpose committee. Here, if the enclosure makes the entire package political, the union will be making an expenditure, and because it is paying the larger portion of the whole the union will be the sender. If the insurance and pension materials, on the other hand, are "regular ongoing business overhead" - including envelope and postage, then the union will be making no "expenditure." The only "expenditures" made will be by the PAC and the mass mailing requirement would seem to apply only to the PAC and its costs. Even if there is an incremental postal charge attributable to the enclosure, paid by the union, the PAC will be paying the larger portion of the political costs and will be the sender.
embedding.summary: The FPPC analyzes a joint mailing by a labor union and its PAC where the union covers non-political materials and postage while the PAC covers political advocacy printing. The response clarifies that only costs directly attributable to political communications count as reportable expenditures, and regular business overhead paid by the union does not trigger sender/disclosure requirements for the union.
